content: A black hole is a region in space where gravity is so intense that nothing, not even light, can escape from it. This happens when a massive star collapses under its own weight after running out of fuel, compressing all its mass into an incredibly small area. The gravity near this point becomes overwhelmingly strong because the mass is concentrated in such a tiny space. Imagine trying to climb out of a pit that gets steeper and steeper the deeper you go—eventually, it becomes impossible to climb out. The boundary around a black hole, called the event horizon, marks the point of no return. Once something crosses this line, the gravitational pull becomes too powerful to resist. While it might sound extreme, black holes are well-supported by decades of observations, such as stars orbiting invisible objects and the detection of gravitational waves from black hole mergers. They’re not science fiction but a natural consequence of gravity and mass in our universe.
